name: Crying Boy Painting
description: The Crying Boy painting is a mass-produced print that gained notoriety in Britain during the 1980s and 1990s due to a series of reports linking it to house fires. The theory posits that the painting is cursed, causing fires to break out wherever it is displayed. The origins of the phenomenon are generally traced to a 1985 article in 'The Sun' newspaper, which reported on a firefighter who claimed to have encountered numerous instances where the only object to survive a house fire was a 'Crying Boy' print. Subsequent reports from other fire services and the public fuelled the belief in a connection between the painting and unexplained fires. Proponents suggest that the print itself possesses a malevolent energy, possibly linked to the unknown artist (often incorrectly attributed to Giovanni Bragolin) or the subject of the painting. Some theories even suggest the boy depicted was orphaned by fire, imbuing the artwork with negative energy. Mainstream explanations for the phenomenon focus on confirmation bias and the print's material composition. The varnish used on the prints might have contained fire-retardant chemicals, leading them to survive fires while other household items were consumed. Furthermore, the print's widespread popularity meant it was statistically more likely to be present in households that experienced fires, making the correlation a coincidence rather than a causal link. Psychological factors, such as suggestibility and the power of suggestion, also contributed to the widespread belief in the curse. While the 'curse' is largely debunked, the Crying Boy painting remains a significant example of urban legend and mass hysteria in British popular culture.
subtitle: Cursed artwork and house fires in Britain.

claims.evidence: Numerous reports from firefighters and homeowners claiming the painting was the only item to survive house fires.
claims.criticism: Confirmation bias; fire-retardant varnish used on the prints; statistical probability due to widespread ownership.

claims.evidence: Anecdotal accounts of bad luck and negative experiences associated with owning the painting.
claims.criticism: Subjectivity; psychological factors such as suggestibility; lack of scientific evidence.

claims.claim: The boy depicted in the painting was cursed or orphaned by fire.
claims.evidence: Speculation and rumors surrounding the identity of the 'Crying Boy' and the artist.
claims.criticism: Lack of verifiable information about the artist and subject; origins of the painting are unclear.

keyFigures.name: Steve Punt and Hugh Dennis
keyFigures.role: BBC Radio 4 comedians who investigated the phenomenon

keyFigures.name: Tom Slemen
keyFigures.role: Liverpool-based author who wrote about the Crying Boy curse in his books

timeline.event: Mass production and distribution of the 'Crying Boy' prints.
Publication of the initial article in 'The Sun' linking the painting to house fires.
Height of the 'Crying Boy' panic in Britain, with numerous media reports and public discussions.
Decline in widespread belief in the curse, with increased focus on debunking and scientific explanations.

sources.author: The Sun
sources.work: Various articles on house fires and the 'Crying Boy' painting
sources.title: The Sun

sources.author: David Clarke
sources.work: Various articles on urban legends and paranormal phenomena
sources.title: David Clarke
